content.text: Arrays and tuples are both sequential collections of values. TypeScript's type system tracks them differently: an array holds any number of elements all of the same type, while a tuple holds a fixed number of elements where each position has its own type.

attrs.alt: Comparison of TypeScript arrays (variable-length, homogeneous) vs tuples (fixed-length, heterogeneous per position)

content.text: generic form is also valid — they produce identical types.
TypeScript types all built-in array methods. The return type of each method flows from the array element type — no annotations needed.
API and many RxJS utilities produce read-only sequences.
A tuple is an array with a fixed number of elements where each position has its own type. TypeScript enforces both the length (at assignment) and the type at each index.
TypeScript 4.0+ supports labelling tuple positions. Labels improve IDE tooltips and error messages without changing the runtime behaviour.
A tuple element can be made optional by appending
. Optional elements must appear at the end of the tuple.
) lets a tuple have a variable-length tail after fixed positions.
TypeScript types all destructured bindings, including rest elements and skipped positions.
Returning a tuple from a function is the TypeScript equivalent of returning multiple values. It is particularly common in React-style hooks and Angular utility functions.
) over anonymous tuples when the positions are not self-evident from destructuring names. Labels appear in IDE tooltips and make the API contract explicit.
Use spread to create a new array
Swap the values to match the declared order
Using Array index beyond tuple length
Add optional element or use an array instead
TS2339 — push doesn't exist on readonly
Accept readonly in param type and spread to mutate caller-side
Forgetting to annotate generic empty array in service
Array.sort() mutates in place and works correctly on strings by default, but requires a comparator for numbers or objects.
You can extract the type of an array element using the indexed access syntax T[number].
as const Arrays — Literal Type Narrowing
Without as const, TypeScript widens array literals to their base types. Use as const to preserve exact literal types.
TypeScript 4.0 introduced variadic tuple types, allowing tuples to be spliced and composed at the type level.

to keep array literals as literal-union types. Use indexed access
to extract union types from arrays at compile time.
tuples for fixed-length, positionally-typed structures like function return pairs or config entries.
: all elements are the same type; length is dynamic
: prevents mutation — use for Angular signal values and read-only inputs
Tuples: fixed length, each position has its own type; label elements for clarity
Destructuring works on both arrays and tuples — TypeScript types each binding from the array element type or the tuple position type
